msgs.text:
I re-read the problem statement and the prior thirteen proposals. The prompt fixes the four-generator tame format with distinguished sigma,tau, implicit tau^sigma = tau^2, later generators in the pro-2 core, and exponents reduced modulo M=85667662080.The prior file’s real surviving thread is the Turn 9–13 “dynamic tame-inertia” candidate; the earlier candidates were refuted by explicit finite tame or index-two subgroup tests, so I did not treat them as viable.

The current candidate remains:variables sigma,tau,x,y definitions nx = (x*tau^50932630783)^40491355905 ny = (y*tau)^40491355905 relations x*sigma^45176306175*x*sigma^40491355905*x^-1*nx*ny^sigma*y^-1

Equivalently, with π=π2 and η=2π on prime-to-2 tame inertia,r=xσ−πxσπx−1(xτ−η)π ((yτ)π)σy−1.

The constants are π≡40491355905,−π≡45176306175,−η=−2π ≡50932630783(modM).The prior Turns 9–13 established substantial finite-tame evidence: correct Frattini module, nondegenerate cup product on both trivial and nontrivial tame-inertia blocks, and the mod-4 orientation class. This is exactly the kind of data that appears in the Jannsen–Wingberg method: Neukirch’s report says the proof for the known local presentations studies, for each finite tame normal subextension, the Demuškin group of the maximal p-extension and the finite tame group action on it, then packages this as a Demuškin/Koch formation whose invariants determine the group.Numdam The dyadic K=Q2 case is genuinely outside Diekert’s covered case, as Jarden–Shusterman explicitly note.Mathematics at TAU My new check was aimed at the remaining dyadic orientation issue, not the already-proved mod-2 cup layer.First, I confirm the correction that must be used in the trivial tame-inertia block. With A=F2 [T]/(Tf−1), s=πmodf, and trivial τ-character, the linear relation is(1+T−s)X+(1+T)Y=0.

When f is even, s is odd, so 1+T∣1+T−s, and the correct elimination is

Y=P(T)X+Yˉ,P(T)=1+T1+T−s.

The reduced multiplier is still h(T)=1+P(T)+P(T−1),and the unit proof uses (1+ζ)h(ζ)=ζ−s+ζs+1.

Thus h(ζ)=0 would force ζ2s+1=1. Since s≡1(mod2a) and s≡0(modf0) for f=2af0, 2s+1 is coprime to f. So the trivial block is indeed nonsingular. This is not a new proof of the cup-product result, but it removes a notation ambiguity in the prior turns.The new computation is one level deeper in the cyclotomic tower. In the invariant base block, the candidate reduces to

D=⟨x,y,z∣xz−1 xz[z,y]⟩.

The mod-4 orientation character from the candidate isκ=x∨+y∨.I computed the Reidemeister–Schreier presentation of V=kerκ, using transversal {1,x}. With Schreier generatorsa=yx−1,b=z,c=x2,d=xy,e=xzx−1,the two abelianized relators reduce over Z2 to rows (0,2,1,0,−2),(0,−2,1,0,2).

Their Smith form is diag(1,4).

So Vab has C4 -torsion and not C8 -torsion, matching L(i) but not L(ζ8).More importantly, I enumerated the 15 index-two subgroups of this V. Their abelianized 2-primary torsion distribution is 14 subgroups with C4,1 subgroup with C8.This is the next cyclotomic step expected for a rank-4 Demuškin group with q=4: among the quadratic extensions of the i-extension, exactly one adjoins ζ8. Labute’s classification makes this the relevant invariant: for q=2, Serre’s character χ and its image Imχ distinguish the dyadic Demuškin types, and two Demuškin groups with the same rank and Imχ are isomorphic.Cambridge University Press & Assessment+1 Labute also records the local-field input: H1 is identified with K×/K×p, the cup product is the Hilbert symbol, and the Hilbert symbol is nondegenerate.Cambridge University Press & Assessment This strengthens Turn 12/13 slightly: the candidate’s invariant block does not merely stop at “C4, not C8 ” for the sign kernel; its next index-two layer has the expected unique C8 branch. Since all non-invariant tame blocks in the Turn 9–13 reduction have 2-adic unit determinants, this recursive torsion behavior is concentrated exactly in the invariant block.I also narrowed the remaining formation gap. For the odd-order part of a finite tame quotient, the equivariant rigidity is essentially formal: the kernel of

Aut(D)→Aut(D/ΦD)for a finitely generated pro-2 group D is pro-2, so an odd-order tame subgroup acting with the already identified H1 -module, cup product, and Bockstein data has a unique lift up to conjugacy by the profinite Schur–Zassenhaus argument. Thus the still-nonformal part is not all of Δe,f; it is specifically the 2-primary unramified Frobenius part, where Schur–Zassenhaus gives no uniqueness. That is the precise place where a dyadic formation lemma is still needed.So I would not mark the presentation as fully proved yet. The finite-tame pro-2 kernels now look correct not only as abstract Demuškin groups but also through the first two cyclotomic layers. However, the final step must still show that the actual lift of the 2-power Frobenius action on the Demuškin kernel is forced by the displayed relation and the distinguished element S=σf, or else directly prove the dyadic Koch-formation uniqueness statement for this candidate.

ESTABLISHED: The Turn 9–13 candidate survives an additional second-level cyclotomic test. In the invariant base block, the sign kernel has Smith factor 4, and its 15 index-two subgroups have torsion distribution 14×C4 and 1×C8, matching the expected μ4→μ8 branch. The odd-order tame action is rigid once the H1, cup, and Bockstein data are fixed.

GAP: The 2-primary Frobenius-action rigidity remains unproved. The remaining missing theorem is a dyadic formation/uniqueness lemma for the tuple (D,σ,S), where D is the finite-tame Demuškin kernel and S=σf∈D.

NEXT: Work modulo D3 and D4 for the 2-power Frobenius lift: prove that an automorphism of the candidate Demuškin kernel with the given action on D/ΦD, the given Bockstein character, and the given f-th power S is unique up to inner automorphism.[BREAKTHROUGH]
